Baylor College of Medicine Endowment has declined to invest in Fund II. The last recorded touch was February 28, 2026, with Tony assigned as the relationship owner. No additional detail is available on the nature of the decline or any prior engagement history.

Tony should look to clarify the rationale behind the decision — whether Baylor's endowment cited allocation constraints, credit strategy misalignment, or simply a capacity issue — as that context will inform how and whether to keep this relationship warm for a future vehicle.

Future Fund::AREC Debt Fund II.narrative:
Future Fund is an active prospect at the Interested stage, with a $200M target allocation and an in-person meeting confirmed for March 17 in Irvine. The last touch was March 5, when Tony's Pacific Palisades meeting distribution included relevant agenda prep for this engagement. Momentum is building toward the March 17 meeting as the primary near-term milestone, and the relationship is flagged as urgent.

Julia McArdle is the primary contact at Future Fund, though her specific role has not yet been confirmed. On the AREC side, Oscar owns the relationship, with David Hardy leading content and presentation development for the March 17 meeting. Glen is also on distribution for agenda prep. The strategic framing — per advice from Greg Kostka and Hillwood — is a strategy-focused pitch rather than a direct Fund II sales presentation, with the team fielding ops (Glen), construction and development (David Hardy), and deals (Kevin) as content areas.

The immediate priority is finalizing the agenda and presentation deck for March 17. That work is assigned to Oscar and must reflect the strategy-first approach Tony has directed. The March 17 meeting is co-scheduled with the Hanwha / Su Man Ahn engagement in Irvine, suggesting a concentrated investor meeting day at AREC's Irvine office.

Future Fund's status as an Australian sovereign wealth fund warrants the careful, relationship-first framing AREC has adopted. At a $200M target, this would be one of the larger institutional commitments in the fund, making the March 17 meeting a high-stakes first formal engagement. The Hillwood/Kostka advisory context suggests AREC has external intelligence shaping how to position the conversation.
Future Fund::AREC Debt Fund II.at_a_glance: In-person meeting March 17 in Irvine; prep underway

Texas Permanent School Fund::AREC Debt F.narrative:
Texas PSF is at the Interested stage following a substantive introductory Teams call on March 5 with Viktor Makaroff, Reid Spears, and Kevin Godin. The call was highly engaged — Viktor led with 25+ detailed questions covering A&D mechanics, leverage, risk, and fundraising timeline — and the overall tone was positive. Reid called the strategy "very interesting" and expressed a desire to continue diligence before dropping off early; Zach sent him the call recording same day. Viktor acknowledged interest but flagged June timing as a challenge and indicated he'd discuss internally. Data room materials and the Mountain House co-invest book were all delivered on March 5. The account is flagged urgent and assigned to Tony.

Viktor Makaroff is the primary diligence lead — 8+ years at PSF with a hedge funds and Chevron M&A background, he ran the meeting and is the key decision-influencer to win over. Reid Spears (18 months at PSF, previously at an Oklahoma-based foundation) is a warm second voice and warrants his own dedicated follow-up given he left early. Kevin Godin attended but his role is still unconfirmed. Nick Tramontana, whose role also remains TBD, is a notable internal figure — he came up on the call when Viktor and Reid asked if AREC had previously met him. He has a Richmond American homebuilder background, which gives him direct familiarity with A&D lending and positions him as a potential internal champion worth cultivating.

The immediate priorities are confirming the proposed in-person Austin visit with Viktor for March 18 or 20, and getting a separate follow-up call on the calendar with Reid. Both are in motion. Viktor has not yet responded to Tony's Austin proposal, making that confirmation the most time-sensitive open item.

The strategic context here is significant: PSF is actively shifting away from standard fund allocations toward $1B+ structured partnerships with equity or revenue share components — potentially an equity stake in TopCo or a multi-tiered, multi-year arrangement. At a $500M target, this is one of the largest prospects in the Fund II pipeline, and the June 30 close timeline is a genuine constraint Viktor flagged. The AREC team should be prepared to engage seriously on the partnership structure angle, not just the fund commitment, as that framing may be what ultimately unlocks PSF's participation. Reid's wife is on UTIMCO's VC team, which represents a potential warm introduction path to Jared Brimberry at UTIMCO worth keeping in mind.
Texas Permanent School Fund::AREC Debt F.at_a_glance: Awaiting Viktor's confirmation on Austin visit March 18/20

Aayaan Mehta.narrative:
Aayaan Mehta is an investor at StepStone Group, the global private markets investment firm. StepStone manages and advises on a substantial book of institutional capital across private equity, real estate, infrastructure, and private debt, making it a meaningful institutional prospect for Fund II. Specific details on Aayaan's title and decision-making authority within StepStone are not on file.

Oscar owns the relationship with Aayaan and has him marked as interested in Fund II at a $30M indication. The most recent logged activity was the delivery of AREC's track record on February 17, 2026, suggesting the conversation is in an early diligence phase. There is a separate StepStone-affiliated relationship in the network — Ashton Newhall, founder of Greenspring Associates, which was sold to StepStone in 2021 and is now connected through the Newhall family office (Brightside). That relationship is owned by Patrick Fichtner and is distinct from Aayaan's coverage, though it may be worth understanding whether there is any internal connectivity at StepStone worth leveraging.

Oscar should follow up to confirm that the track record materials were received and assess whether Aayaan has had time to review them. Given the $30M indication, the priority is understanding StepStone's internal process — whether Aayaan is the decision-maker or if this goes to an investment committee — and what additional materials or diligence steps are needed to advance. The Greenspring/Newhall connection to StepStone is worth keeping in mind as context, though care should be taken not to conflate the two relationships without clearer visibility into how StepStone is organized internally.
